title: Episode 5

jokes.text: I know I seem mental, but I'm fine.
jokes.setup: Fleabag opens by acknowledging she appears unstable
jokes.punchline: The immediate reassurance 'I'm fine' undercuts itself — the self-awareness of seeming mental while insisting on fine-ness is the joke
jokes.explanation: Classic Fleabag self-deprecating opener — the joke is in the self-aware contradiction. 'I seem mental but I'm fine' is the character's thesis statement. Strong economy, highly character-specific, works as both comedy and characterization.

jokes.text: I stole that sculpture from a certain somebody's studio.
jokes.setup: A conversation about a missing art piece and the Finland promotion; Fleabag has been playing along as if innocent
jokes.punchline: Casually confesses to theft mid-conversation as if it's a minor detail
jokes.explanation: The casual dropping of a major moral transgression into polite conversation is quintessential Fleabag — dark, economical, and entirely character-specific. The 'certain somebody' phrasing adds a layer of absurd coyness that makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Your husband tried to kiss me on your birthday.
jokes.setup: Stepmother is lecturing Fleabag not to let other people get in the way of what she wants; the scene is building emotional tension
jokes.punchline: Fleabag drops a bomb about Martin as if it's a conversational trump card — the timing is brutal and the content is devastating
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: The line lands as both shocking confession and dark comedy because of its devastating timing — it punctures the Stepmother's self-righteous speech at its peak. The specificity ('on your birthday') makes it worse and funnier simultaneously. Waller-Bridge's ear for when comedy and horror are the same thing is on full display. The follow-up beat (Did he?) is the perfect capper.

jokes.text: Did he?
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just told Stepmother that her husband (Martin) tried to kiss her on her birthday
jokes.punchline: Stepmother's response is a single, flat 'Did he?' — not outrage, not tears, just a bored, knowing acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: Two words that do more work than any monologue could. The response is funny because it's SO unsurprised — revealing volumes about Martin, about this marriage, about this woman's life, all in a single bored query. The comedic economy is near-perfect. This is a 'the room goes silent then erupts' moment. The joke IS the understatement — she already knew, or at minimum isn't shocked, which is darkly hilarious and kind of devastating.

jokes.text: Yeah, evil boobs everywhere.
jokes.setup: Doctor solemnly informs Fleabag that her father has informed him of the family's medical history (breast cancer)
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's casual summary of inherited cancer risk as 'evil boobs everywhere'
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: Grief-as-gallows-humor is Fleabag's signature mode, and this is a perfectly calibrated example. Reducing a family history of cancer and a mother's death to 'evil boobs everywhere' is shocking, funny, and deeply in character — it's how Fleabag emotionally survives. The phrase 'evil boobs everywhere' is genuinely original and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Fleabag laughing during breast exam — 'I'm sorry, I'm just ticklish.'
jokes.setup: Doctor is performing a serious breast cancer screening
jokes.punchline: Fleabag breaks the solemn medical moment by laughing and apologizing for being ticklish
jokes.explanation: Functional cringe comedy — the ticklishness breaks the medical gravity in an embarrassing way. Not particularly original but earned within the scene.

jokes.text: Dad books us boob appointments once a year, to make sure our tits don't turn on us like Mum's did.
jokes.setup: Fleabag narrates to camera the ritual of these annual screenings
jokes.punchline: 'to make sure our tits don't turn on us like Mum's did' — personifying cancer as a betrayal by one's own body with dark comic precision
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: 'Tits don't turn on us like Mum's did' is superb Fleabag — it anthropomorphizes cancer as treachery, which is both funny and gutting. The use of 'turn on us' frames the betrayal in terms of loyalty, which is darkly original. This is Waller-Bridge at her best: the line is laugh-out-loud while simultaneously being a devastating portrait of grief.

jokes.text: Bet you look forward to seeing Claire. A lot more to touch, if you know what I mean.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is mid breast exam; she's established the Doctor sees both sisters
jokes.punchline: Implies the Doctor enjoys examining Claire's larger breasts — an extremely inappropriate thing to say to a man examining your chest
jokes.explanation: Fleabag's compulsive need to make things awkward and sexual — even in serious medical contexts — is a character-comedy staple of the show. The Doctor's throat-clearing reaction is the punctuation.

jokes.text: HE CLEARS HIS THROAT — Doctor's only response to Fleabag's inappropriate 'lot more to touch' comment
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just made an extremely inappropriate sexual observation about his enjoying examining Claire's breasts
jokes.punchline: The Doctor's only response is a prim throat-clear — professional dignity barely maintained
jokes.explanation: A perfectly executed reaction beat — the throat-clear is funnier than any verbal comeback could be. The Doctor's decision to say nothing is the most professional and simultaneously most comedic choice.

jokes.text: it's nice to be touched — Fleabag's reason for not minding the annual screening
jokes.setup: Fleabag explains the annual screenings are 'a bit of a hassle'
jokes.punchline: 'But, at the end of the day, it's nice to be touched' — a devastating joke about loneliness disguised as a throwaway line
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is one of the best jokes in the episode and one of the finest examples of Fleabag's signature mode. It's simultaneously the funniest and saddest line in the scene — she's grateful to be touched during a cancer screening because she's that starved of human contact. The joke EARNS the laugh and then makes you feel terrible about laughing. It's thematically resonant (the 'touch' theme recurs throughout the episode), economical, and entirely character-specific. Near the 8.0 anchor in quality.

jokes.text: I check for cancerous lumps in mammary glands. Now, any pleasure I derive from that is entirely dependent upon whether or not I am about to save your life.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has made repeated inappropriate sexual observations during a breast exam; Doctor has been politely absorbing them
jokes.punchline: The Doctor's measured, professional dignity cracks just enough to deliver this perfectly constructed rebuttal — earnest, slightly defensive, very funny
jokes.explanation: A brilliant secondary character joke — the Doctor's logic is airtight and slightly absurd. The phrase 'entirely dependent upon whether or not I am about to save your life' is both genuinely funny and weirdly moving. He's not denying pleasure; he's contextualizing it. Masterful deadpan from a minor character.

jokes.text: Yeah, you left me on a fucking Silent Hill.
jokes.setup: Claire has been waiting outside for Fleabag for ten minutes
jokes.punchline: Describing the location as a 'fucking Silent Hill' — a horror video game reference deployed with complete straight-faced fury by Claire
jokes.explanation: The unexpectedness of Claire using a gaming reference, delivered with her characteristic tight-lipped rage, is the joke. It humanizes her oddly while staying true to her uptight character.

jokes.text: I see it more as a sport. — Fleabag on visiting Dad being 'hell' for Claire
jokes.setup: Fleabag says visiting Dad is hell for Claire
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals she personally enjoys the family dysfunction as entertainment
jokes.explanation: Efficient character establishment — Fleabag's predatory delight in family chaos is perfectly captured in one short line. Not exceptional but well-crafted economy.

jokes.text: Everything's totally fine. / It sounds like it's fine.
jokes.setup: Claire insists everything is fine with Martin, saying it twice with increasing brittleness
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's perfectly dry 'It sounds like it's fine' — agreeing sarcastically while Claire is clearly falling apart
jokes.explanation: A well-executed ironic echo — the triple repetition of 'fine' escalating and then Fleabag's dry agreement is timed beautifully. Classic comedy of the obvious denied.

jokes.text: Let's just get out of this alive, OK?
jokes.setup: The sisters are steeling themselves to ring the doorbell for their mother's memorial lunch
jokes.punchline: Describing a family lunch as a survival situation
jokes.explanation: Functional family-dysfunction comedy. The sentiment is relatable, Claire's delivery (implied) is dry, but it's not particularly original.

jokes.text: Oh, they're actually for Dad. — Fleabag correcting Stepmother's assumption about the flowers
jokes.setup: Stepmother assumes Fleabag brought flowers as a kind gesture to her
jokes.punchline: Fleabag pleasantly corrects her — they're for Dad, not her
jokes.explanation: A small passive-aggressive swipe at Stepmother that is funnier for how casually it's delivered. Sets up the dynamic economically.

jokes.text: DISTANT CHAINSAW BUZZES — immediately upon entering the house
jokes.setup: The family has just gathered for a memorial lunch — a somber, civilized occasion
jokes.punchline: The immediate sound of a chainsaw from the garden creates an absurdly menacing ambience
jokes.explanation: The sound design joke works beautifully — walking into a memorial lunch to the sound of a chainsaw is absurd and sets a perfect tone for the chaos to follow.

jokes.text: Who's Dad sawing in half? — Oh, just the tree.
jokes.setup: Chainsaw sound; Fleabag asks who Dad is sawing in half
jokes.punchline: Claire's flat 'Oh, just the tree' — treating 'sawing someone in half' as a reasonable first guess
jokes.explanation: Strong economy — Claire's non-reaction to the dark framing is the joke. The exchange works because of how neither sister treats 'sawing someone in half' as an outrageous suggestion.

jokes.text: Oh, I hope you don't mind my being here, but my Pilates fell through, so...
jokes.setup: The occasion is a memorial lunch for the girls' dead mother
jokes.punchline: Stepmother explains her presence at her predecessor's memorial as a Pilates scheduling conflict — as if she only came because she had nothing better to do
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is exceptional character comedy — the Stepmother's casual obliviousness is perfect. The 'Pilates fell through' as the REASON she's attending her predecessor's memorial is both deeply funny and surprisingly revealing of her self-centered worldview. The 'so...' trailing off makes it worse and funnier. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor in its specificity and quotability.

jokes.text: It's a sad day. A sad, sad day. — followed immediately by 'I'll get the champagne.'
jokes.setup: Dad solemnly acknowledges the sadness of his late wife's memorial
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivots to fetching champagne — undermining his own gravitas
jokes.explanation: The comedy of deflection — Dad can't sit in the sadness for more than a beat. Functional but not particularly original.

jokes.text: TOTAL SILENCE — then Fleabag to camera: '(This is my favourite bit.)'
jokes.setup: The family is sitting in excruciating silence after Dad has gone to get champagne, waiting for someone to speak
jokes.punchline: Fleabag breaks the fourth wall to tell the audience she loves this specific torture
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is a masterclass in fourth-wall comedy. The silence itself is the setup; Fleabag's private glee is the punchline. The joke works on multiple levels: it's funny, it reveals her character (she survives through spectator detachment), and it shows that the awkwardness we're about to watch is something she actively savors. The whispered aside '(This is my favourite bit.)' is perfectly timed, economical, and deeply character-specific. Among the episode's best.

jokes.text: I love your hat. — It's a hair scarf. — Looks like a hat. — Well, it's a hair scarf. — OK.
jokes.setup: Attempt at pleasant small talk about Claire's hair accessory
jokes.punchline: Claire's stubborn insistence on the distinction between 'hat' and 'hair scarf' despite zero practical difference — the exchange ends in stalemate
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: The hat/hair scarf exchange is beautiful miniaturist comedy. Claire's rigid need to correct a harmless misidentification — twice — tells you everything about her character. The final 'OK' from Fleabag is the perfect beat of weary sisterly non-engagement. Extremely character-specific and efficiently told.

jokes.text: Oh, no. He's a different one. Ooh. You do turn over fast!
jokes.setup: Stepmother is excited to meet Fleabag's 'new chap' whom she assumes is the same as a previous one
jokes.punchline: Learning it's already a different man, Stepmother cheerfully blurts 'You do turn over fast!' — rude, true, and delivered without malice
jokes.explanation: The Stepmother's cheery obliviousness continues — she delivers what might be an insult with complete guilelessness. Solid character comedy.

jokes.text: Dad will come in with some weird canapés in a second. — Girls!
jokes.setup: Fleabag predicts Dad's entrance like she's narrating a nature documentary
jokes.punchline: Dad immediately fulfills the prophecy — 'Girls!'
jokes.explanation: A quick and clean Fleabag-as-omniscient-narrator joke. The prediction coming true immediately (with the canapé detail presumably payable visually) works well.

jokes.text: You're both looking very healthy, very... very good and healthy and...
jokes.setup: Dad is trying to compliment his daughters at a gathering; the context of just-had-breast-exams hangs in the air
jokes.punchline: He keeps looping on 'healthy' and 'good' in a way that feels inadvertently body-focused given the breast exam subtext
jokes.explanation: Dad's inability to say anything to his daughters without awkwardness is well-established British family comedy. The trailing off resonates with the breast exam subplot.

jokes.text: You are my... ..daughters. HE COUGHS.
jokes.setup: Dad appears to be working up to something emotional
jokes.punchline: What he arrives at, after a pregnant pause, is simply '...daughters' — the most obvious possible fact delivered as if it were a revelation, followed by a cough
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is a masterpiece of British emotional repression rendered as comedy. The pause before 'daughters' sets up the expectation of something meaningful; 'daughters' is the most technically-true but emotionally-empty thing he could say. The cough as punctuation is perfect. The sisters' 'Yep. Yes, mmm-hmm.' in response compounds it. Deeply character-specific and resonant.

jokes.text: She always made them sound so rude... The squirrel voices were the best. / Run! Fucking run!
jokes.setup: Dad is attempting to give a memorial speech about Mum
jokes.punchline: What's remembered most fondly is that she made park animals swear — the sisters erupt into 'Run! Fucking run!' reenactment
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: A genuinely touching and genuinely funny moment simultaneously. The idea that Mum's legacy is profane squirrel and pigeon impressions is both funny and emotionally honest — it's how grief actually works. The eruption of 'Run! Fucking run!' from the whole family is warm and chaotic and real. This is Fleabag at its best: laugh-while-your-heart-breaks territory.

jokes.text: Yes, yes, my ex had a similar thing — voices and fun. Really, really funny man.
jokes.setup: The family is having a rare genuine moment of warm memory about Mum
jokes.punchline: Stepmother interrupts to compare Mum to her ex-boyfriend — an extraordinary intrusion into a grief moment
jokes.explanation: The Stepmother's social obliviousness reaches new heights — she makes the memorial about her ex-boyfriend. Character-perfect and effectively cringeworthy.

jokes.text: Plaits. Either she's got her period or some serious shit's gone down.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is watching Claire, noticing something different about her appearance
jokes.punchline: She's developed a precise diagnostic system for Claire's emotional state based solely on hairstyle choices
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is a gem of sibling-knowledge comedy — Fleabag's ability to read Claire's internal state from a hairstyle detail is both funny and oddly touching. The binary diagnosis ('period OR serious shit') is perfect in its simplicity. The follow-up explanation about PMT and 'monthly confidence crisis' expands it beautifully. This is the kind of specificity that elevates TV comedy.

jokes.text: Mum used to call it her monthly confidence crisis. But it's PMT.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has explained Claire's PMT-triggered reinvention rituals
jokes.punchline: The correction '— But it's PMT' after the charming maternal euphemism is perfect — Mum poeticized it; Fleabag is bluntly practical
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: Two sentences that do triple duty — they're funny (the deflating correction), they reveal character (Fleabag vs. Mum's respective voices), and they keep Mum present as a distinct personality. 'Monthly confidence crisis' is a genuinely great coinage; '— But it's PMT' is the perfect undercut. Economy at its finest.

jokes.text: (I'm doing a wee on this cushion.) / What?!
jokes.setup: Fleabag addresses the camera with an aside while sitting at the lunch
jokes.punchline: She tells the audience she is urinating on the cushion; another character apparently hears her and reacts
jokes.explanation: The aside-heard-by-others is a meta fourth-wall comedy device — the parenthetical being intercepted is a twist on the format. 'I sort of wish you were' from someone is a great non-reaction. Funny and playful.

jokes.text: The sculpture / stolen artwork confrontation — 'I don't want it in my house.' / '(I'm doing you a massive favour.)' / 'Come on, what's she going to do? Draw me?'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has apparently brought back (or is being confronted about) the stolen sculpture at this family gathering
jokes.punchline: 'What's she going to do? Draw me?' — a wonderfully dismissive line about the artist's possible response to having her work stolen
jokes.explanation: The callback to the stolen sculpture plus 'what's she going to do, draw me?' is a sharp, confident line — Fleabag's breezy attitude toward her own criminality is funny. The artist-retaliation joke is a neat little absurdist twist.

jokes.text: Why are we sneaking? / We're not allowed upstairs. / Of course we are.
jokes.setup: The sisters are sneaking upstairs in their childhood home
jokes.punchline: The revelation that they're 'not allowed upstairs' in their own childhood home — Godmother has imposed household rules in Dad's house, which Fleabag refuses to accept ('Of course we are')
jokes.explanation: Small but revealing joke — Godmother has essentially colonised their childhood home. The comedy is in the absurdity of adult children being 'not allowed upstairs' and Fleabag's cheerful refusal to comply.

jokes.text: I keep forgetting that she's actually talented. / I know, it's infuriating.
jokes.setup: The sisters are looking at Stepmother's artwork upstairs where they're not supposed to be
jokes.punchline: They're both irritated that the woman they dislike is genuinely good — 'I know, it's infuriating'
jokes.explanation: A sharp observation about the specific frustration of having to credit someone you resent. 'It's infuriating' lands because it's honest and specific to sibling/family dynamics.

jokes.text: Where's her head? / She's got your boobs — she doesn't need one.
jokes.setup: They're looking at a sculpture that apparently lacks a head but is anatomically endowed
jokes.punchline: 'She's got your boobs — she doesn't need one' — a sibling burn delivered while examining their Stepmother's erotic art
jokes.explanation: A compact and well-constructed punchline — 'she doesn't need one' is the right beat length, and the specific context (Stepmother's erotic sculpture, sibling competitive dynamics, the absurdity of the situation) makes it land harder than it would in isolation. Good economy.

jokes.text: (Do you think she's ever painted Dad naked?)
jokes.setup: They're in Stepmother's studio looking at her art
jokes.punchline: Fleabag whispers the thought that's obvious but that everyone is politely not thinking
jokes.explanation: A predictable thought rendered slightly funnier by being whispered as an aside. Not particularly original but earns a chuckle in context.

jokes.text: He hates being alone in a room with me. Watch this.
jokes.setup: Fleabag is observing that her father can't be alone with her without discomfort
jokes.punchline: She sets it up like a nature documentary trick and then executes it
jokes.explanation: The 'watch this' fourth-wall invitation is effectively used here — Fleabag as ringmaster of the family chaos. The setup is clean.

jokes.text: Dad's panicked salt-searching: 'Oh! Uh...oh... Ah...erm... I just need some... Just need... I need some... salt. There it is.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just entered the kitchen; Dad needs to flee but can't without a reason
jokes.punchline: After a series of panicked false starts, the escape excuse he arrives at is 'salt' — located immediately at the end of his great performance of need
jokes.explanation: Superb character comedy — Dad's absolute inability to handle emotional proximity rendered as a physical comedy of avoidance. The escalating stammering resolved by 'salt' is perfectly calibrated. 'There it is.' as relief is the cherry on top.

jokes.text: Have you got enough... clothes?
jokes.setup: Dad asks Fleabag how she is; it comes out as concern about whether she has enough clothes
jokes.punchline: The most banal possible expression of parental care — 'clothes' — as the thing Dad can ask about when he clearly can't ask about her actual life
jokes.explanation: A beautifully observed moment of British paternal inadequacy — asking if someone has enough clothes when you can't ask how they're really doing is specifically, painfully funny and true.

jokes.text: We've just been keeping it quiet. We haven't been able to buy anything or do anything... What's that? Oh, that? That's just a tiny little house we're buying in France.
jokes.setup: Dad has just told Fleabag they're 'tight on the purse strings' when she mentions struggling financially
jokes.punchline: He is immediately caught looking at paperwork for a French house they're buying — a perfectly self-incriminating contradiction
jokes.explanation: The timing of this reversal is impeccable — 'tight on purse strings' to 'tiny little house in France' in the space of two lines is devastating class-comedy that also reveals character (Dad's self-deception). 'Tiny little house' is the perfect minimizing phrase.

jokes.text: Ooh...What's that? / Oh, that? That's just a...tiny little house we're...we're buying in...in France.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been telling Dad about the cafe's financial struggles. Dad has just implied they're 'tight on the purse strings.'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag spots something — presumably a brochure or property detail — and Dad reveals they're buying a house in France. The 'tiny little house' is doing enormous work as a euphemism; its tripled diminutives ('tiny little house') against 'in France' is perfectly comic.
jokes.explanation: The comedy is the hypocrisy caught in real time — Dad said 'tight on the purse strings' while apparently buying a French property. The stammer of 'tiny little house we're...we're buying in, erm...in France' shows him aware of how bad this looks and unable to manage it. Solid dramatic irony structured as comedy.

jokes.text: Oh, no! / Jesus, Dad! Again? / She mustn't see it. She mustn't find out. / Mmm, five second rule. / No, I have never bought into that rule. It's disgusting.
jokes.setup: Dad has apparently dropped something (canapés, papers relating to the exhibition) on the floor and is panicking about Stepmother finding it
jokes.punchline: The absurd domestic crisis of dropped canapés plus Fleabag's 'five second rule' versus Dad's emphatic rejection of the rule as 'disgusting'
jokes.explanation: Functional physical comedy — the five-second rule gag is a known quantity but Dad's extremely firm rejection of it is specific and funny. The 'Again?' from Claire implies this is a recurring Dad behavior, which adds depth.

jokes.text: I mean, I didn't want to show off, but...
jokes.setup: The hot boyfriend (Bus Rodent / Hot Priest equivalent here — her date) has arrived at the door
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's understated brag to camera about how attractive he is
jokes.explanation: A clean and economical fourth-wall aside. Not exceptional but efficiently funny — the 'I didn't want to show off' as prelude to showing off is a nice self-aware beat.

jokes.text: Gosh... You really are... — the family's reaction to the boyfriend's looks
jokes.setup: The very attractive boyfriend has arrived; various family members are struggling to be normal
jokes.punchline: Incomplete sentences of stunned appreciation — 'You really are...' trails off without finishing
jokes.explanation: The unfinished sentence is the joke — they can't even form a compliment coherently. Functional visual comedy that sets up the running 'very good-looking' gag.

jokes.text: Fucked me up the arse. — as how they met
jokes.setup: Stepmother asks the social pleasantry 'So, how did you two meet?'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag answers with a stark sexual confession, then her date smoothly recovers with a gentler cover story
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is one of the episode's true standout moments. The social convention 'how did you meet' is one of the most over-scripted questions in existence; Fleabag's answer is the single most anti-social possible response, and then her date's immediate pivot to finding her crying in a toilet somehow makes both stories true. The callback at 21:21 ('I totally see that now') elevates it further. Maximum funny per word — four words that stop the room.

jokes.text: Actually, you know — and I can say this because I'm an artist — but you really are very good-looking. Thank you. Very. Thank you. Very. Thank you. I mean, almost... ..too good-looking.
jokes.setup: Stepmother has established she's an artist and therefore has special visual authority
jokes.punchline: The triple 'Very. Thank you.' exchange, escalating to 'almost... too good-looking' as if there's a problem
jokes.explanation: The escalating repetition is well-timed and the 'almost... too good-looking' is a genuinely funny absurdist beat — suggesting there's a threshold beyond which attractive becomes a character flaw.

jokes.text: Well, if you'll excuse me, I'm going to go and do that old human thing.
jokes.setup: The Stepmother has been intensely staring at him and calling him almost too good-looking
jokes.punchline: His exit line — 'that old human thing' — is gloriously vague and slightly alien-sounding, as if going to the bathroom is a biological novelty
jokes.explanation: A wonderfully eccentric line — 'that old human thing' as a euphemism for using the toilet is both absurdist and slightly self-mocking (as if he's aware of his own preternatural handsomeness and the weirdness of being observed). Good character comedy for a secondary character.

jokes.text: Oh, and again... THEY LAUGH — second time someone has apparently spilled/knocked something
jokes.setup: Dad has previously dropped something; 'again' implies he's done it again
jokes.punchline: The 'again' and the identical laughter response to the recurrence
jokes.explanation: Functional running gag payoff — the recurrence earns a smile but it's not doing complex work. The joke is simply that Dad keeps dropping things.

jokes.text: It's a 'sexhibition.'
jokes.setup: Asked about her exhibition
jokes.punchline: The portmanteau 'sexhibition' — delivered with complete seriousness
jokes.explanation: The word 'sexhibition' is genuinely funny — it's exactly the kind of thing this character would coin and be proud of. The earnestness of the delivery amplifies it. A good character-comedy pun.

jokes.text: simply a journey through my physical and sexual life, climaxing in a few pieces inspired by and moulded on your father
jokes.setup: The girls have asked what the sexhibition involves
jokes.punchline: 'climaxing in a few pieces... moulded on your father' — the double meaning of 'climaxing' and the revelation that Dad's body is the artistic centerpiece
jokes.explanation: The accumulated horror of this speech is expertly crafted — 'climaxing' is used correctly but sexually, and 'moulded on your father' confirms that the girls' father's body is the exhibition's centerpiece. This is high-grade cringe.

jokes.text: Why? — Well, I think it's important for women of all ages to see how my body has changed over the years. I think they have to have a healthy perspective on my body.
jokes.setup: Stepmother has revealed she's photographed herself naked every year for 30 years
jokes.punchline: Her reasoning is that women need a 'healthy perspective on MY body' — framing her own body as a public health resource
jokes.explanation: The narcissism here is perfectly calibrated — the Stepmother genuinely believes women need to see HER body for their health. The 'my body' emphasis is the comedic bull's-eye, suggesting a solipsism so extreme it becomes comedic.

jokes.text: I mean, I don't need to tell you, but your father is a deeply sexual man. / No, you don't. / Just did.
jokes.setup: Stepmother says she 'doesn't need to tell' the daughters that their father is a 'deeply sexual man'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'No, you don't' followed by Stepmother's cheerful 'Just did' — a perfect comedy of the obvious transgression self-identified
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: Three words ('Just did.') that are the perfect punchline to one of the episode's great setups. The comedic structure is immaculate: the setup is 'I don't need to tell you,' the implicit contract of that phrase is that she won't tell them — and she immediately, cheerfully, violates it and acknowledges doing so. The awareness ('just did') makes it funnier than simple obliviousness. This is the Stepmother character at peak perfection.

jokes.text: CAT MEOWS — as punctuation after 'I will be touched until the day I die. And so will you, Claire.'
jokes.setup: Stepmother has delivered an increasingly unhinged speech about being 'touched until the day I die'
jokes.punchline: The cat meow as comic punctuation — a moment of absurd bathos
jokes.explanation: Sound-design comedy — the cat meow deflating the grand speech is a functional but low-originality device. The cat (Felicity) is a running element but this particular beat is mild.

jokes.text: Tell Dad about your promotion, Claire. / There's nothing to tell. / Finland. / Oh! / Odd place.
jokes.setup: Fleabag weaponises her knowledge of Claire's Finland promotion (which Claire told her not to reveal)
jokes.punchline: Godmother's response to 'Finland' is 'Odd place' — an utterly inadequate and slightly dismissive reaction to a major career announcement
jokes.explanation: 'Odd place' as a response to career news is a perfect Godmother non-response — she's not interested in Claire's achievements unless they're about her. The callback to Claire's promotion lands correctly.

jokes.text: Because you CAN'T just fuck off on aeroplanes, and leave your weird stepson and broken sister to fend for themselves, OK?
jokes.setup: The table is pressuring Claire about why she's not taking the Finland promotion
jokes.punchline: Claire's explosion reveals that 'broken sister' is her reasoning — she can't leave Fleabag, and she describes her thus with brutal honesty
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: 'Weird stepson and broken sister' is a spectacular double-descriptor — the specificity of 'weird' for the stepson and 'broken' for Fleabag is both funny and heartbreaking. The outburst reveals Claire's entire emotional situation in one furious sentence. It's character-specific (only Claire would use this particular vocabulary under pressure), structurally excellent (the pressure builds across the table until this), and the comedy comes from the honesty of 'broken.'

jokes.text: I'm just so annoyed with myself! I wish I could just... meet myself and just... have a go at myself!
jokes.setup: Claire has just had a breakdown/outburst at the table and is now alone with Fleabag
jokes.punchline: The image of wanting to physically 'have a go at' herself — Claire's frustrated desire to self-confront is rendered as wanting to fight herself
jokes.explanation: A wonderful absurdist expression of a very real feeling — the fantasy of self-confrontation expressed as physical violence against yourself. Very Claire-specific (she'd channel frustration into physical action). Funny and touching.

jokes.text: Do your worst. Come on, bitch!
jokes.setup: Claire has just said she wants to 'have a go at' herself; Fleabag apparently re-enters and offers herself as a proxy
jokes.punchline: 'Come on, bitch!' as a self-directed invitation — Fleabag volunteers to receive the verbal punishment on Claire's behalf
jokes.explanation: A fast, well-executed callback — Fleabag offering herself as a punching bag for Claire's self-directed frustration is both funny and oddly sisterly. 'Come on, bitch!' is a great line.

jokes.text: You do not take yourself seriously as a businesswoman, you need to pay your fucking bills, you need to be nicer to Hillary and you need to get a new hat!
jokes.setup: Fleabag has invited Claire to 'do her worst'; Claire launches into a genuine tirade of specific criticisms
jokes.punchline: The escalation from serious life criticisms to 'you need to get a new hat' — the last item on the list is the most trivial and the funniest
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is a beautifully constructed list joke with escalation in reverse — from 'you don't take yourself seriously' (meaningful) to 'get a new hat' (trivial). The callback to the hat/hair-scarf exchange makes it funnier. The 'be nicer to Hillary' is also great — one specific name in a list of general failings. The structure (serious → serious → specific → absurd) is textbook comedy list-writing.

jokes.text: Is that better? / Yeah, it's better.
jokes.setup: Claire has just delivered a comprehensive roast of Fleabag's life
jokes.punchline: Both sisters acknowledge it was therapeutic — 'Yeah, it's better' — with complete matter-of-factness
jokes.explanation: The agreement that verbal assault was therapeutic is funny in its matter-of-factness. The economy is strong — the two-line exchange does all the work.

jokes.text: The artwork in this house is...stunning. / Who is it? / If I tell you, will you promise to come to my sexhibition?
jokes.setup: Boyfriend has been looking at the art and compliments it; Godmother seizes the opportunity
jokes.punchline: 'If I tell you, will you promise to come to my sexhibition?' — Godmother uses Boyfriend's art appreciation as leverage to extract a sexhibition commitment
jokes.explanation: Good character comedy — Godmother's transactional use of art-identity to extract social obligations is revealing. The callback to 'sexhibition' lands cleanly. The absurdity of leveraging art attribution for a sexhibition invite is perfectly Godmother.

jokes.text: If I tell you, will you promise to come to my sexhibition?
jokes.setup: Boyfriend admires the art in the house and asks who made it
jokes.punchline: Stepmother using 'sexhibition' again — now as leverage, like a salesperson
jokes.explanation: The second deployment of 'sexhibition' as a social currency — the Stepmother using it as a bargaining chip with the attractive man is consistent with her character. Solid running gag.

jokes.text: How's your little restaurant? / It's a cafe. / Oh, don't do it a disservice. / I'm not. It's a cafe.
jokes.setup: Stepmother asks about Fleabag's business using a slightly wrong word
jokes.punchline: Fleabag corrects her; Stepmother insists the correction is self-deprecating; Fleabag insists it isn't — an exact mirror of the hat/hair-scarf exchange
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is a structural callback to the hat/hair-scarf exchange — both are conversations about the correct name for a thing, both feature someone insisting on the correction being wrong, both end in the same stubborn standoff. The symmetry is satisfying, and the fact that it's Fleabag on the receiving end (rather than giving it to Claire) adds a layer. The Stepmother's 'Oh, don't do it a disservice' is a particularly inspired character moment — she thinks Fleabag is being humble.

jokes.text: Oh, her dear little friend died, and left her to run the cafe on her own. / (Jesus.) / Oh, God, that is truly awful. How did she die? / Oh, she killed herself. / It was an accident.
jokes.setup: Stepmother casually discloses Fleabag's trauma to the boyfriend in Fleabag's presence; the death of Fleabag's friend is a central unresolved grief
jokes.punchline: The contradiction: 'She killed herself.' / 'It was an accident.' — two different framings of the friend's death, neither comfortable
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: This is the darkest and most structurally complex joke in the episode. The rapid-fire contradiction — 'she killed herself' vs. 'it was an accident' — is both funny (the reflex denial, the cover-up impulse) and devastating (the ambiguity about how Boo died, Fleabag's guilt). It's a comedy that earns its laugh by being completely honest about something terrible. The Stepmother's oblivious disclosure is the crowbar that opens it. The '(Jesus.)' to camera between lines is perfect timing.

jokes.text: We did this. — Mmm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Stepmother is suggesting Fleabag give up the cafe
jokes.punchline: Fleabag pulls the boyfriend aside and says 'We did this' about the cafe — a declaration of ownership and creation that is also an implicit 'no'
jokes.explanation: Two words of quiet determination — 'We did this' — carries emotional weight and a gentle comedy of understated pride. The 'Mmm-hmm' from the boyfriend is perfect minimal support.

jokes.text: Well, if you rid a woman of her head and limbs, you can't expect her to do anything other than...roll around.
jokes.setup: Godmother is discussing the headless sculpture that 'turned up' (the one Fleabag stole)
jokes.punchline: 'If you rid a woman of her head and limbs, you can't expect her to do anything other than...roll around.' — simultaneously art commentary, darkly feminist observation, and absurdist logic
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line — it works as genuine art philosophy, as dark comedy about female body objectification (a limbless woman can only roll around), and as absurdist logic simultaneously. The pause before 'roll around' is crucial to the delivery. One of the episode's best lines and one of Godmother's best moments. Genuinely original, highly quotable, earns 8.5.

jokes.text: Well, if you rid a woman of her head and limbs, you can't expect her to do anything other than... roll around.
jokes.setup: The sculpture that was missing has turned up — it apparently 'toppled off the side'
jokes.punchline: The Stepmother's philosophical observation about headless, limbless women rolling being inevitable
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent absurdist line — the logic is internally consistent ('without limbs, she can only roll') and the delivery ('you can't expect her to do anything other than...roll around') is perfectly structured. It also functions as a commentary on the sculpture (and perhaps on women generally) that gives it extra resonance.

jokes.text: Your father and I often say, when you've had a few drinks, you're so like your mother.
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been laughing at the rolling sculpture joke
jokes.punchline: Stepmother casually deploys the most double-edged compliment possible — comparing Fleabag to her dead mother, in the context of having drinks at her memorial
jokes.explanation: The comedy here is in the layers — it's both a compliment and an intrusion, delivered at a memorial for the mother being evoked, by the woman who replaced her. The pause (indicated by the ellipsis) before this line presumably shows Fleabag's face, which is the real punchline.

jokes.text: Oh, whoops. — Fleabag apparently stumbles or knocks something after the emotional mother comparison
jokes.setup: She's just been told she's like her dead mother while looking at the headless-woman sculpture
jokes.punchline: The 'oh, whoops' functions as both physical comedy and emotional deflection — Fleabag breaks the tender moment with clumsiness
jokes.explanation: The physical comedy here functions primarily as emotional avoidance — a Fleabag hallmark — but it's mild and largely dependent on the visual performance.

jokes.text: Look at these. — the family looking at photos (presumably Stepmother's yearly naked photographs)
jokes.setup: Stepmother has described taking a naked photo of herself every year for 30 years
jokes.punchline: She apparently produces them at the party — 'look at these' — sharing her naked yearly progression with the assembled family gathering
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the yearly nude photograph project — she's actually sharing them. The social horror of this is well-built, and the 'look at these' nonchalance is perfectly Stepmother.

jokes.text: Does anyone mind if I leave? I've got a dicky tummy. / So swift...
jokes.setup: The naked photos are apparently being circulated; the boyfriend makes his exit
jokes.punchline: His exit excuse is 'dicky tummy' — politely British; Fleabag's 'So swift...' is a fond, amused acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: The boyfriend's tactical retreat from the naked photo situation is funny in its politeness — 'dicky tummy' is a magnificently British excuse. 'So swift...' is good deadpan from Fleabag.

jokes.text: I'm not going to kiss you because I'm probably very ill.
jokes.setup: The boyfriend has invented a 'dicky tummy' to flee the naked photo viewing
jokes.punchline: Committing fully to the fiction by refusing to kiss the host goodbye for health reasons
jokes.explanation: The dedication to maintaining the fake illness is funny — he's being a good sport and a good actor. British politeness as comedy.

jokes.text: Goodbye then, my... ..my daughters.
jokes.setup: The party is ending; Dad is saying goodbye
jokes.punchline: He again trails off and arrives at '...my daughters' — a callback to 'You are my... ..daughters' earlier, same emotional vacancy
jokes.explanation: The callback works — Dad ends the gathering exactly as he began it, with the same inability to say anything meaningful beyond the factual. The repetition crystallizes his emotional limitation as a character note.

jokes.text: Yeah. See you at the sexhibition.
jokes.setup: They're saying their goodbyes
jokes.punchline: 'See you at the sexhibition' as a casual farewell — said with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The 'sexhibition' becomes a farewell ritual — said easily and without embarrassment. A nice running gag payoff.

jokes.text: No, there's absolutely no need to... / Yes, lovely. / No, no - I'll definitely be there. / I will definitely be there.
jokes.setup: Dad is trying to diplomatically get out of attending the sexhibition
jokes.punchline: Both Claire and Fleabag independently and enthusiastically commit to attending the sexhibition over Dad's protests — united for the first time all episode, against Dad's discomfort
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the sisters' solidarity — their shared delight at tormenting Dad with sexhibition attendance is a warm moment of sisterly bond expressed through mischief. Dad's 'absolutely no need' protestations make it better.

jokes.text: It's been really nice to spend the day with a normal family.
jokes.setup: They've just spent a day with a family that included: a chainsaw, a sexhibition, Dad's France house, the 'killed herself/it was an accident' moment, naked yearly photos, and stolen art
jokes.punchline: He sincerely, straightfacedly calls them 'a normal family'
jokes.explanation: The irony is so thick it's structural — the audience has just watched the entire episode and this line is the culmination of all of it. 'A normal family' after everything that has occurred is a beautiful summative joke. The sincerity of delivery is crucial — it's funnier if he means it.

jokes.text: Erm...what's his...? / Fucked me up the arse. / Oh. / Yep. It's... / Mm-hmm. / I totally see that now.
jokes.setup: Claire is about to ask Boyfriend's name; Fleabag gives the same answer she gave Godmother earlier
jokes.punchline: 'I totally see that now.' — Claire looking at the departing Boyfriend and acknowledging that his apparent personality is exactly what Fleabag described
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes of the episode. The callback structure is formally perfect: (1) Fleabag confides to camera how they met at 12:22, (2) she uses the same line again when Claire asks, (3) Claire's response 'I totally see that now' confirms that it's a character description, not just a sexual fact. The economy is extraordinary — 'I totally see that now' does everything. Claire's understanding is entirely visual (she's looking at him) and completely comic. This is the kind of callback that justifies the entire setup. Clearly 8+ territory.

jokes.text: What's his...? / Fucked me up the arse. / Oh. / Yep. It's... / Mm-hmm. / I totally see that now.
jokes.setup: Claire didn't catch how they met earlier; Fleabag repeats the line
jokes.punchline: 'I totally see that now' — Claire, after spending time with the man, now completely understands the anal sex explanation of how they met
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: The callback to the episode's best one-liner, now delivered to Claire, with Claire's delayed-but-complete understanding ('I totally see that now') is the funniest payoff in the episode. The joke is perfect on structure: first time is shocking, second time builds on established character reaction (Claire's acceptance vs. shock), and the punchline is Claire's calm endorsement. The brevity and the specificity of 'I totally see that now' is exactly what makes callbacks great.

jokes.text: I'm going to leave Martin. / I'm going to give you the money for the cafe, / and I'm going to go to fucking Finland.
jokes.setup: The sisters have had their moment of connection; Claire has been building to something
jokes.punchline: Claire delivers three life-changing decisions in rapid succession, ending with 'fucking Finland' — the profanity landing on 'Finland' being the comedy, and 'fucking' transforming a geography into an expression of liberation
jokes.explanation: The episode's emotional climax and one of its best jokes. 'Fucking Finland' earns its profanity because it's Claire — controlled, restrained Claire — swearing on a country name as an act of defiance. The three-part structure (Martin/money/Finland) builds to the expletive like a speech. The comedy is entirely dependent on Claire's character: any other character saying this would be half as funny. High quotability, massive callback payoff, genuine character breakthrough. Comfortably 8+.

jokes.text: Oh, and, erm... — followed by apparently revealing she took the sculpture — That is the coolest thing you've ever done. / I know.
jokes.setup: Claire has just made several life-changing declarations; Fleabag adds one more piece of news (the sculpture theft)
jokes.punchline: 'That is the coolest thing you've ever done. I know.' — Claire endorsing the theft as Fleabag's crowning achievement
jokes.explanation: Justification for 8+: A satisfying structural callback that closes the sculpture arc with warmth and wit. Claire calling a theft 'the coolest thing you've ever done' is a great character beat — coming from the controlled, rule-following Claire, this endorsement is funnier and more affecting. 'I know' from Fleabag is perfect confident understatement. The episode ends on a genuine sisterly warmth that the comedy has earned.

jokes.text: Shall we...? / We can try.
jokes.setup: The sisters are about to part; there's an implied emotional moment (hug? connection?)
jokes.punchline: 'We can try.' — Claire's characteristically guarded response to what is presumably a request for physical or emotional connection; neither refusing nor promising
jokes.explanation: 'We can try' as response to a request for connection is perfectly Claire — she cannot commit to warmth but she won't reject it. The hesitation is the character. Tender and funny simultaneously.

jokes.text: I'll see you at the sexhibition.
jokes.setup: Final scene — the sisters have said their emotionally meaningful goodbyes
jokes.punchline: The final line of the episode is 'I'll see you at the sexhibition' — the word that has become the episode's comic motif lands as both a joke and, somehow, a touching promise
jokes.explanation: The 'sexhibition' as the final word of the episode is structurally satisfying — a running joke elevated to an emotional beat. It's simultaneously absurd and warm, which is Fleabag's signature register. The episode ends on a laugh-cry note.

tmdb_overview: Fleabag and Claire return to their family home for the annual memorial lunch.
